what_it_does: This file is a design note that maps the annotation/comment pipeline (select → comment → batch → submit → read replies → choose) as an end-to-end voice-first circuit, pairing each step with a touch fallback and surfacing two requirements not covered by the touch brief: text-to-speech reply output and constrained-vocabulary voice for closed choices.
observations: A 9-step table pairs a voice-first path with a touch role for each stage of the pipeline.
Voice triggers include 'comment on this', 'the whole section', 'change request —', 'attach a screenshot', 'send all', and option labels.
Long-press/tap anchors are treated as eyes-free or fallback scale pickers.
Live transcript is shown on screen during dictation, with keyboard as correction fallback.
References other design items with BD-* labels: BD-D, BD-H, BD-F, BD-F/G, BD-E.
Two explicit gaps are raised: replies must be readable aloud via TTS, and choices/options should use constrained-grammar recognition.
It distinguishes closed-vocabulary commands from free-dictation comment prose as a VUI reliability principle.
Accessibility scenarios include walking, one-handed use, eyes-occupied tasks, and visual fatigue.
standouts: Replies and decision questions should be readable aloud on demand via TTS, creating an output channel not scoped elsewhere.
Closed choices/options are the cleanest voice win because recognition can be constrained to known option labels.
Constrained-vocabulary voice should be used for all closed command sets; free dictation reserved only for actual comment prose.
conventions: Uses a structured markdown table to compare voice and touch modalities.
References sibling design items using BD-* identifiers and a board:// URI.
Follows the noticeboard item schema with id, address, type, links, timestamps, and markdown body.
Written as a design brief/requirement rather than implementation code.
concerns: Claims constrained grammars are 'near-error-free' without addressing real-world noise, accents, or disambiguation.
Does not mention error recovery, confirmation, or cancellation paths for voice commands.
Assumes TTS and ASR availability without noting platform constraints or privacy implications for voice notes.
No definition of the 'V-overlay' or 'member' terms used in the voice loop.
notes: A voice-first design walkthrough of the comment pipeline that insists on TTS replies and constrained-grammar recognition for closed choices.
questions: Which component or platform provides the ASR/TTS capabilities?
How is the constrained grammar bound to the recognizer at runtime?
What exactly is the 'V-overlay' and how does it relate to the touch UI?
How does 'send all' resolve ambiguity when multiple comments are queued?
What is the BD-* numbering scheme and where are the referenced BD-D/BD-H/BD-F/BD-E items defined?
How are misrecognized constrained commands corrected or undone?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches; the stated purpose is to walk the pipeline as a voice circuit and note mandatory touch, and the file does that while also surfacing TTS output and constrained-vocabulary choices.
intention_signals: It is trying to establish voice as a primary modality for the entire annotation loop, ensure touch remains a usable fallback, and push two overlooked voice requirements—spoken replies and constrained-choice recognition—into the design scope.
novelty: It treats the whole comment pipeline as a single voice circuit rather than adding voice to isolated steps, and it explicitly separates closed-grammar commands from free-dictation prose as a reliability strategy.
contribution: A voice-loop mapping for the annotation pipeline, a requirement for on-demand TTS of replies, and the principle of using constrained-vocabulary recognition for closed commands and choices while reserving free dictation for prose.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design item that maps the end-to-end comment pipeline as a voice-first circuit with touch fallbacks and argues for TTS reply output plus constrained-grammar voice recognition for closed choices.
intention_for_embedding: To surface voice-first interaction requirements and gaps for an annotation system, particularly spoken replies and constrained-vocabulary command recognition.
